text: Hi, this is Dr. Mario Leba. I present to you a difficult case that I recently did. Patient had a prior history of tram flap and surprise mesh finding. I ended up doing an ETEP with unilateral tar with some tedious dissection along the way.

I tend to stay away from tram flap patients as the bulge is not going to get better. But in this case, There definitely was a defect and the patient was symptomatic. I looked at the CT scan and thought it potentially may be feasible. I did discuss with her all the potential complications and or outcomes at the end, which may or may not improve her symptoms. She elected to proceed.

The surgeon gains access to the retroactive space on the patient's right side using a blunt trocar under direct visualization. The space is created, ports are placed, and the robotic system is docked to begin the crossover procedure.

based on my initial quick peak port, identified that there was actually mesh in the abdomen, which I did not realize and the patient did not tell me. So I knew I was going to encounter some mesh during the crossover. In addition to that, I knew it was going to be somewhat difficult as the posterior sheath may be disrupted along with the tram flap subsequent changes and likely difficult dissection of that retroactive space. on the contralateral side.

The surgeon evaluates the crossover area, identifying the rectus muscle by its color change. The striations of the linea alba are visible, abruptly ending at the location being shown, suggesting a potential area for crossover.

The surgeon observes the absence of the rectus muscle in the area, revealing fat tissue due to the lack of an anterior sheath. The right side of the medial aspect of the linea alba is clearly defined, with visible striations. Muscle fibers, likely residual from prior surgery, are present as the dissection continues.

The surgeon identifies the mesh in the medial location and decides to approach dissection above it to avoid the peritoneum. A vessel is identified and preserved, potentially related to the tram flap. The surgeon finds a suitable plane and continues dissection along the linea alba, maintaining alignment to avoid deviation.

The surgeon assesses the lateral extent of the dissection, avoiding neurovascular bundles that may have been compromised. The dissection is guided by the proximity to a previously identified vessel, ensuring not to injure the semi-lunar line or penetrate the skin, which is visibly close.

Probably the most difficult part of the entire case, I thought, was the management of the very thin peritoneum. There's a lot of surgeons who want to do tap, I'm sorry, who want to do E-tept, but are not very familiar with the tap dissection and the concepts of peritoneal recruitment and gentle dissection, and this absolutely was present in this case. Majority of it was tap surgery and not necessarily E-tap surgery.

The surgeon uses a quick peak port to drain or vent air, providing a visual window into the abdomen. Through this port, the hernia defect is visible, along with the very thin peritoneum. The surgeon carefully reduces the hernia sac to avoid dealing with a peritoneal rent.

What was interesting to me during this surgery was the fact that there was two linear strips of mesh placed and I'm almost wondering if they had injured the the dissection or had some injury during the dissection and had to repair some of these holes because as you see here there's essentially nothing above and I was thinking maybe they're trying to cover up that space and I'm not sure if this was a consequence of the surgery and the tram flap itself with injury of the fascial tissues in that region. However, it made it a little bit more challenging trying to figure out what was normal.

The surgeon carefully elevates the edge of the mesh with their left hand while using Kettner dissectors to start the TAR plane. The mesh is adhered to the tissue, causing occasional tears. The dissectors, resembling caterpillars, help soak up blood and maintain visibility of the dissection plane.

The surgeon achieves good mobilization of the peritoneum, navigating past troublesome areas. The mesh is visible, with disrupted anterior tissue planes. Intra-abdominal evaluation through the rent aids in maintaining spatial awareness of the surgical location.

In the pelvic region, the surgeon encounters difficulty locating the epigastric vessels, which are typically used to guide the trajectory towards the inguinal region. Several surgical clips are visible along the base, near the round ligament, indicating previous vessel sacrifice.

The surgeon closes all visible holes and measures for mesh placement. The surgical field shows a defect with only subcutaneous tissue and thin skin above. Attempts to close the mesh to the surrounding tissue result in tearing due to poor tissue quality, leading to the decision to abort further closure attempts.

Here I was just debating if I was gonna close the diastasis, and I just thought it'd add tension to it, so I decided not to do it. I've not done this in the past. This was a unique case. Others who've had ETEPs with maybe a tar on tram flap patients, I'd like to get your input on what you did. I really think there was some sort of injury at the initial surgery, and that's why they put those pieces of mesh in there, so the tissue planes were gonna be disrupted anyways.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field for hematomas, observing a puddle of fluid without major holes. The assistant PA is seen fashioning the mesh while the surgeon uses suction to clear the area.

The surgeon places the mesh tailored to fit the defect size, deploying VistaSeal for hemostasis and securing the mesh without additional fixation. The abdomen is insufflated, revealing intact peritoneum and posterior sheath. A laparoscopic tap block is performed circumferentially on both sides to allow VistaSeal adhesion and suction placement.
